rows.title: Tennessee HB 1898: Consumer Protection Provisions for AI-Generated Voice and Likeness
rows.summary:
The Tennessee General Assembly passed House Bill 1898, the Ensuring Likeness Voice and Image Security (ELVIS) Act, amending the state's existing personal rights protection framework to address the unauthorised use of an individual's voice and likeness by artificial intelligence systems, with the legislation applying across Tennessee.

On 21 March 2024, Governor Bill Lee signed HB 1898 into law, with the Act taking effect on 1 July 2024. The legislation amends the Tennessee Personal Rights Protection Act of 1984 to extend protections against the unauthorised reproduction or simulation of an individual's name, photograph, voice, and likeness to cover AI-generated replications, filling a gap in the prior statute that had not contemplated generative AI technologies.

The Act provides that no person may use an AI system to produce a simulation of an individual's voice or likeness for commercial purposes without prior consent from that individual or their authorised representative. The legislators determined that existing right-of-publicity protections required updating to reach the distribution of AI-generated audio or visual content that mimics a real person's identity. The Act further provides that liability extends to those who knowingly distribute or transmit such AI-generated content, not solely the party that created it, broadening the scope of actionable conduct compared with the 1984 predecessor statute.

The ELVIS Act introduces a private right of action, allowing affected individuals to seek injunctive relief and damages. The Act provides that prevailing plaintiffs may recover actual damages or statutory damages, as well as attorneys' fees in successful actions. The legislation was developed with particular reference to the music industry, as Tennessee is home to a substantial recording and performance sector, and the legislators identified AI voice cloning of recording artists as a pressing concern driving the amendment. The Act does not create an exemption for commentary, criticism, or parody that would otherwise qualify under established First Amendment doctrine, though existing constitutional protections remain applicable as a matter of federal law.

rows.title: Tennessee HB 1898: Consumer Health Data Privacy Legislation
rows.summary:
Tennessee House Bill 1898 is a consumer health data privacy measure introduced in the Tennessee General Assembly, establishing requirements for the collection, use, and sharing of personal health information by regulated entities operating in Tennessee.

On introduction in the Tennessee General Assembly, HB 1898 was referred to the relevant committee for consideration. The bill addresses the processing of consumer health data by entities that are not covered under the federal Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), thereby extending privacy protections to a broader category of health-related information held by commercial actors.

The bill defines consumer health data to include information that identifies or is reasonably linkable to a consumer and relates to the consumer's physical or mental health status, including data derived from health-related products and services. Under the bill's provisions, regulated entities are required to obtain affirmative consent from consumers before collecting or sharing consumer health data for purposes beyond those necessary to provide a requested product or service. The bill further requires entities to publish a consumer health data privacy policy disclosing the categories of data collected, the purposes of collection, and the categories of third parties with whom data is shared.

HB 1898 grants consumers specific rights with respect to their health data, including the right to access the data held about them, the right to request deletion of that data, and the right to withdraw previously given consent. The bill provides a private right of action for consumers whose rights are violated, as well as enforcement authority for the Tennessee Attorney General. Entities found to be in violation are subject to civil penalties as prescribed in the bill's enforcement provisions.

The bill draws from a model developed by Washington State's My Health MY Data Act and reflects a pattern of state-level legislative activity aimed at filling perceived gaps in federal health privacy law, particularly with respect to consumer-facing applications, wearable devices, and digital health platforms that fall outside HIPAA's scope.
